text: Welcome to James Cook University Hospital where here we present a uniportal right vats pericardial window.
This lady has a very long history of tuberculosis and has had renal TB and many years ago had a left VAT window and a sub-xiphoid window.
These had closed and for the last five or six years she's had a gradually collecting pericardial effusion that was causing some minor shortness of breath.
Thus we elected to perform a right VAT pericardial window.
This is the single port that we've placed.
Unfortunately, and perhaps expectedly, we did find that there were some considerable adhesions in the chest.
Although left with the lack of other options for a window by minimally invasive techniques, we elected to continue by taking down the adhesions.
Passing a long black curved diathermy to the side of the single porthole, we commenced a somewhat lengthy adhesiolysis.
We in fact managed to pass several instruments around the side of the single porthole, including kitners, suction devices,

and also a novel device that you'll see shortly there's us pressing down actually seeing the pericardium and in the near ground is just the part of the lower lobe so at two o'clock there you can now see the quite distended pericardium this is over the right atrium now as the observant of you might have noticed marks the reamer vein just above the pericardium there with some diathermy marks
We've just placed a needle and aspirated to find straw-coloured fluid.
Now we're using that novel device I told you about.
This is the Cavidian Sills Dissector.
It opens and closes, but it's also got a full range of curved motion.
You can just see the curved part of it changing angle, which is very easily achieved.
from outside of the uniport.
So we're just entering the pericardium by hooking and lifting up.
I know from the needle that there's a quite significant amount of fluid so there's no danger of breaching the right atrium.
So I'm hooking and lifting up and diatherming the first incision through the pericardium assisted by the SILS dissector which has lifted up the pericardium also.
I've also got a kitner at 12 o'clock
which is just retracting on a sucker below, all around the single port.
So the sucker has been advanced into the pericardium and I can now start to create my 2cm, or in fact larger in this case, window.
So I'm now advancing the Sills Dissector, changing the curve of it as I go to protect the right atrium, to increase the size of the window.
And you can see the right ventricle in the distance.
So I'm lifting up the pericardium, you can see the heart below, a bit of right ventricle, and I'm continuing the window.

In uniportal VATS procedures, this angled SILS dissector is particularly useful so that it can move away from the single line of vision, which allows better vision for the second working device, which in this case is the diathermy.
I've now just created the window.
I wanted to send off some pericardium for TB analysis and we've sent off some fluid already from the needle aspiration.
This is the final part of the window and this is withdrawn through the incision and inspected.
because of her previous past history, I did want to extend and increase the size of this window.
So with good vision of the heart and the suction device protecting the heart, I just diathermed the window open somewhat.
It should be noted that on the right-hand side there's very little risk of heart herniation in contrast to the left-hand side.
And you can see all there, the slightly thickened, scarred right atrium and ventricle is obvious.
There's no chance of cardiac protrusion.
And I'm just looking in the pericardium just for any evidence of modular lesions or anything else that might want biopsy.
But nothing was found in particular of note.
So that's the creation of our very large uniportable VATS window.
A drain was inserted to encompass both the pericardium and the right chest cavity and the procedure was finished.
The drain was left in for three days and then withdrawn and the patient discharged pain-free.
And there's the external view.
of our single port, using Westridge Act to extend slightly the single incision.
